Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI → AI governance and regulation

Lesson 3 of 3

AI governance and regulation

EU AI Act, GDPR implications, model documentation, risk assessment, and organizational AI governance frameworks.

25-35 min AI/ML flow AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

AI regulation is rapidly evolving. Engineers must understand compliance requirements to avoid legal liability and build systems that meet regulatory standards.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Regulatory landscape EU AI Act categorizes systems by risk level with corresponding requirements2. Documentation and auditing Model cards document intended use, limitations, and evaluation results3. Organizational governance Cross-functional review boards bring diverse perspectives to deployment decisions

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing AI regulation is rapidly evolving. Engineers must understand compliance requirements to avoid legal liability and build systems that meet regulatory standards. 2. Regulatory landscape EU AI Act categorizes systems by risk level with corresponding requirements3. Documentation and auditing Model cards document intended use, limitations, and evaluation results4. Organizational governance Cross-functional review boards bring diverse perspectives to deployment decisions
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

AI regulation is rapidly evolving. Engineers must understand compliance requirements to avoid legal liability and build systems that meet regulatory standards.

What to say first

  • Understands the risk classification system in the EU AI Act.
  • Can produce model documentation meeting regulatory requirements.
  • Knows how to conduct an AI impact assessment for a new system.
2

Study section

Regulatory landscape

The EU AI Act, GDPR right to explanation, US executive orders, and sector-specific regulations create compliance requirements for AI systems.

Call these points out explicitly

  • EU AI Act categorizes systems by risk level with corresponding requirements
  • High-risk AI systems require conformity assessments and documentation
  • GDPR Article 22 grants rights around automated decision-making
3

Study section

Documentation and auditing

Model cards, data sheets, impact assessments, and audit trails provide the documentation required for compliance and accountability.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Model cards document intended use, limitations, and evaluation results
  • Datasheets describe dataset composition, collection process, and known biases
  • Impact assessments evaluate risks to fundamental rights before deployment
4

Study section

Organizational governance

AI ethics boards, review processes, incident response, and responsible disclosure frameworks operationalize AI safety at scale.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cross-functional review boards bring diverse perspectives to deployment decisions
  • Staged deployment with escalation paths controls risk during rollout
  • Incident response plans define how to handle AI system failures and harms

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Understands the risk classification system in the EU AI Act.
  • Can produce model documentation meeting regulatory requirements.
  • Knows how to conduct an AI impact assessment for a new system.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Treating AI governance as purely a legal concern without engineering involvement.
  • Producing documentation as a box-ticking exercise without meaningful content.
  • Ignoring international regulatory differences when deploying globally.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you classify a resume screening system under the EU AI Act?
  • Design a model card for an LLM-powered medical triage assistant.
  • What technical measures would you implement to comply with GDPR Article 22?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Bias detection and fairness AI safety, ethics, and responsible AIExplainability and interpretability AI safety, ethics, and responsible AI

AI study companion

Go beyond the overview with practical examples

Use the highlights below to study the lesson more deeply, then walk through runnable examples and system-design drills.

0 code examples 2 design drills

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Understands the risk classification system in the EU AI Act.
  • Can produce model documentation meeting regulatory requirements.
  • Knows how to conduct an AI impact assessment for a new system.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Treating AI governance as purely a legal concern without engineering involvement.
  • Producing documentation as a box-ticking exercise without meaningful content.
  • Ignoring international regulatory differences when deploying globally.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • How would you classify a resume screening system under the EU AI Act?
  • Design a model card for an LLM-powered medical triage assistant.
  • What technical measures would you implement to comply with GDPR Article 22?

Design drill

Write a model card

beginner

Create a complete model card for a loan approval ML model following the Model Cards framework. Cover intended use, limitations, ethical considerations, and performance across demographic groups.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What information should the model card include about training data and methodology?
  • How do you document performance across different demographic groups?
  • What limitations and failure modes should be explicitly stated?
  • How do you specify intended vs out-of-scope use cases?
  • What governance processes should be documented for model updates?

Design drill

Conduct an AI risk assessment

intermediate

Perform a structured risk assessment for deploying an AI-powered medical triage system. Identify risks, likelihood, impact, and mitigation strategies.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What are the highest-severity failure modes (false negatives for critical conditions)?
  • How do you assess risk for different patient demographics?
  • What regulatory frameworks apply (FDA, EU AI Act, HIPAA)?
  • Design mitigation for each high-risk scenario (human oversight, confidence thresholds, escalation paths)
  • How do you ensure ongoing compliance as the model and regulations evolve?

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "AI governance and regulation" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • How would you classify a resume screening system under the EU AI Act?
  • Regulatory landscape
  • Documentation and auditing
  • Understands the risk classification system in the EU AI Act.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants

Bring your own model
